title: Device Translation Table Syntax
text: Device Translation Table Syntax A device translation table contains delimiters that mark the beginning and end of the table and sections within the table. Each section contains mapping definitions that define an output code sequence or character, the corresponding Uniface meta code (a function mnemonic or meta character), and optional description or comments. Explain this code begin_table {^^CommentText} begin_device meta_Variant^display | ^printer {Description} DeviceControlDefinitions end_device ^begin_set {CharacterSetNumber} {^CHR{/8} FONT_Number {Description} } OutputCharacterDefinitions ^end_set {^begin_font UnifaceFontDefinitons ^end_font} end_table Copy Delimiters Delimitor Description Details begin_table end_table Start and end of the device translation table; not visible in the editor. All text after the end_table mnemonic is ignored. begin_device end_device Start and end of the device section of the table, which contains the DeviceControlDefinitions for many common functions. Device Section begin_set end_set Start and end of each character set section. Each table can define up to 128 character sets (numbered 0 through 127) per device. Character Set Section begin_font end_font Start and end of the optional font section, which defines information about characters in the Uniface fonts, that is the meta character sets. Font Set Section Arguments ^^ CommentText —two carets ( ^^ ) anywhere on a line indicate a comment line. It is not necessary to use ^^ in the Description or after a begin_device, end_device, begin_set , or end_set code . It is recommended that you add comments to translation tables. Meta_Variant —identifies the installed character set and type of the translation table. Description Each definition in the device translation table consists of a line containing up to 1024 characters, divided into three columns, separated by spaces or tabs. The sum of all output sequences (device control code, characters, and fonts) in the device translation table must not exceed 9000 bytes. Example: MSWIN3 The MSWIN3 is the default device translation table for display in Windows environments. (...) indicated information that has been left out for the sake of brevity. ^BEGIN_DEVICE Meta_Western display ^NUL MODE_0(80,25) Disable mode 0 ^END_DEVICE ^BEGIN_SET 0 ^^ ANSI Character Set ^CHR FONT_0 ^CHR/8 FONT_1 ^127 FONT_2 ^127 FONT_3 ^127 FONT_4 ^127 FONT_5 ^127 FONT_6 ^127 FONT_7 ^189 VIEW_HALF ^187 VIEW_HT ^164 VIEW_FF ^182 VIEW_CR ^169 VIEW_CC ^145 0.^96 ^32 1.^32 ^32 2.^32 ^185 2.^49 ^^ ... <snip> ^184 3.^39 ^32 4.^32 ^128 5.^126 ^130 5.^116 ^131 7.^118 ^132 5.^117 ^^ ... <snip> ^142 2.^94 U017D LATIN CAPITAL Z WITH CARON ^145 5.^121 ^146 7.^41 ^^ ... <snip> ^156 3.^112 ^158 2.^126 U017E LATIN SMALL Z WITH CARON ^159 2.^92 ^^ ... <snip> ^END_SET ^BEGIN_SET 1 ^^ OEM Character Set (CodePage 437) ^178 VIEW_FRAME ^4 VIEW_MENU ^12 1.^42 ^11 1.^58 ^17 4.^90 ^27 4.^91 ^^ ... <snip> ^158 7.^117 ^159 7.^118 ^END_SET ^BEGIN_SET 2 ^^ Symbol Character Set ^230 2.^33 ^232 2.^34 ^^ ... <snip> ^116 4.^115 ^117 4.^116 ^102 4.^117 ^99 4.^118 ^121 4.^119 ^119 4.^120 ^113 4.^121 ^228 7.^45 ^END_SET Copy Related Links Device Translation Tables Device translation tables handle output to screens and printers. They map Uniface logical functions onto device functions, making it possible for Uniface to support a wide variety of hardware platforms, including their output devices. Define a New Translation Table Define your own device translation table if you want to modify how a device works, or address a device that is not yet supported. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
